core.intro: Peaches are one of the most rewarding backyard fruit trees in Australia, but the secret to a good crop is choosing a variety that suits your winter chill and then keeping leaf curl in check. The state sections below cover the climate, growing regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes here apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Peaches come in white or yellow flesh, and as clingstone (the flesh grips the stone) or freestone (the flesh lifts away cleanly). A nectarine is the same species, simply a smooth-skinned peach, so it grows the same way. You can also get flat or doughnut types (Angel, China Flat), full-sized traditional varieties (Anzac, Elberta, Golden Queen, O'Henry), early low-chill types (Flordaprince, Flordagold, Tropic Snow), and genetic-dwarf or columnar trees (Trixzie Pixzee, Crimson Rocket) bred to stay small in a pot. Decide on flavour and use first, then match the variety to your climate.

sections.heading: Matching the variety to your winter chill
sections.body: Peaches are deciduous and need a spell of winter cold to wake up and crop properly. Chill is counted as hours below about 7 degrees, and varieties differ widely: low-chill types need only around 150 to 300 hours and suit warm and coastal districts, while traditional varieties such as Golden Queen need 600 hours or more. Plant a high-chill variety where winters are too mild and you get patchy, delayed budburst and a poor crop, so matching the variety's chill needs to your district is the single most important choice you make.

sections.heading: Pollination: one tree is enough
sections.body: Peaches are self-fertile, so a single tree will set a full crop with no second variety needed. The well-known exception is J.H. Hale, which has sterile pollen and must have another peach nearby to fruit. For almost every other variety, one tree on its own is fine, which makes peaches an easy choice for a small garden.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Give peaches full sun and deep, free-draining soil at a slightly acid pH of about 6.0 to 6.5. They are very sensitive to wet feet, so never plant in a low spot where water pools, and lift the tree onto a low mound if your soil is heavy clay. Being deciduous, bare-rooted trees are sold and planted while dormant in winter (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Pruning and thinning
sections.body: Unlike apples, peaches fruit on wood that grew the previous summer, so they need firm pruning every winter to keep producing fresh fruiting wood, trained to an open vase shape that lets in light and air. In spring, thin the young fruit to roughly a hand-width apart: it feels brutal, but it gives larger peaches and stops heavy limbs snapping under the load. Skip the annual prune and the fruiting wood moves to the tips, leaving bare, unproductive branches lower down.

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Water deeply and regularly from flowering through to harvest, especially while the fruit is swelling and the stones are hardening, because a thirsty tree drops fruit and ripens small. Apply a nitrogen feed in early spring as growth begins, and mulch to hold moisture in the soil, but ease off heavy feeding late in the season so the wood hardens before winter.

sections.heading: Harvest
sections.body: Peaches ripen over summer, and the Australian season as a whole runs from about mid-September to April depending on variety and district. Pick when the background colour shifts from green to yellow or cream, the fruit gives slightly to a gentle squeeze, and it smells fragrant. Peaches gain little sugar after picking (some firm, non-melting types barely soften at all), so leave them on the tree until they are nearly ready.

sections.heading: Leaf curl and other troubles
sections.body: Peach leaf curl, a fungus (Taphrina deformans) that puckers and reddens the new spring leaves, is the disease every peach grower meets. Timing is everything: spray copper or lime sulfur at bud swell in late winter, before the buds open, because once the leaves are out it is too late for that season. Brown rot can rot flowers and fruit in warm, wet spells near harvest, and fruit fly is the critical summer pest in most parts of the country.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted tree rather than a seedling: peaches do not come true from seed, and a grafted tree fruits sooner and to type. Named varieties are worked onto peach rootstocks such as Nemaguard, with plum-based rootstocks used for heavier or wetter ground. Bare-rooted trees in winter are the cheapest way in, dwarf and columnar forms suit pots and small gardens, and above all pick a variety whose chill needs match your climate. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties available right now and what they cost.

faqs.q: Do I need two peach trees to get fruit?
faqs.a: No. Peaches are self-fertile, so a single tree will set a full crop on its own. The one common exception is J.H. Hale, which has sterile pollen and needs another peach nearby; almost every other variety fruits fine alone.

faqs.q: How much winter chill does a peach need?
faqs.a: It depends on the variety. Low-chill types need only about 150 to 300 hours below 7 degrees and suit warm or coastal areas, while traditional varieties need 600 hours or more. Choose one that matches your district, or you get poor budburst and a thin crop.

faqs.q: Why are my peach leaves curling and turning red?
faqs.a: That is peach leaf curl, a fungus called Taphrina deformans. Spray copper or lime sulfur at bud swell in late winter, before the buds open. Once the leaves have emerged it is too late to control it for that season.

faqs.q: Can I grow a peach tree in a pot?
faqs.a: Yes. Choose a genetic-dwarf or columnar variety such as Trixzie Pixzee or Crimson Rocket; they stay compact and will crop in a large container in full sun with regular water and feeding.

WA.intro: Growing peaches in Western Australia. The cooler hills and southern districts give the winter chill that peaches want, while the warm Swan coastal plain suits low-chill varieties, and WA's quarantine rules shape where you can buy a tree.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: South-west WA has warm, dry summers that ripen peaches well, but winter chill varies a lot across the region. The coastal plain around Perth is mild, so it favours low and medium-chill varieties, while the Darling Range and the cooler Southern Forests carry enough chill for traditional types. Pick the variety to suit your part of the state rather than the other way around.

sections.heading: Where peaches grow in WA
sections.body: The orchard district of Donnybrook in the south-west is WA's best-known stone-fruit and apple area, and the Perth Hills around Karragullen, Pickering Brook and Bickley grow stone fruit close to the city. The cooler Southern Forests around Manjimup and Pemberton carry more winter chill, while the warm Swan Valley ripens early. WA's commercial fruit belt runs in a broken line from Gingin north of Perth down to Albany on the south coast.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA's warm summers bring peaches on steadily. Low-chill varieties on the Swan coastal plain ripen from late spring, while the hills, Donnybrook and the Southern Forests run through summer and into autumn, giving the state a long season across its districts.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: Mediterranean fruit fly is endemic across WA and is a primary pest of peaches and other soft stone fruit, so most growers net trees or bait for it. WA is free of Queensland fruit fly, a prohibited pest that is eradicated whenever it is detected, so Medfly is the fly to plan around here. Leaf curl still appears in wet winters and springs, so keep up the late-winter copper spray.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA quarantine means live plants must be permitted on the Western Australian Organism List and are usually certified and treated before they can enter the state, and stone fruit (Prunus) nursery stock is tightly controlled for biosecurity. Many eastern nurseries therefore will not post peach trees to WA, so the in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship here.

faqs.a: On the warm coastal plain, choose low-chill varieties such as Flordaprince, Flordagold or Tropic Snow. In the cooler Perth Hills and Southern Forests you can also grow higher-chill traditional types like Golden Queen and Anzac.

faqs.q: Why won't some nurseries post peach trees to WA?
faqs.a: WA quarantine restricts live stone fruit (Prunus) plants entering the state. Trees must be permitted on the WA Organism List and usually certified and treated first, so many eastern-states nurseries simply choose not to ship plants here.

QLD.intro: Growing peaches in Queensland. The Granite Belt has the winter chill for traditional peaches, while the warm coast and subtropics need low-chill varieties bred for mild winters.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Coastal and subtropical south-east Queensland, and the tropics further north, are low in winter chill, so traditional high-chill peaches fail there. Low-chill varieties, bred from the University of Florida program, made warm-climate peach growing possible and are the right choice for most of Queensland. The Granite Belt is the cool exception where traditional varieties also thrive.

sections.heading: Where peaches grow in QLD
sections.body: The Granite Belt around Stanthorpe and Applethorpe, high on the New England Tableland at around 800 metres with real winter chill and frost, is Queensland's deciduous-fruit district. Low-chill peaches are grown in the warmer South-East and Moreton districts, the Burnett, and on the Atherton Tableland in the far north.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Coastal low-chill peaches are among the earliest in Australia: harvest starts in September and the main crop runs from mid-October to the end of November. Up on the Granite Belt, traditional high-chill varieties ripen later, through summer from December to February.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is native throughout eastern Australia and is the key pest of peaches in the state, so netting, baiting and prompt clean-up of fallen fruit are essential. Queensland's humid summers also favour brown rot of the fruit and leaf curl in spring, so a fungicide program matters more here than in dry inland districts.

faqs.q: Can you grow peaches on the Queensland coast?
faqs.a: Yes, but only low-chill varieties such as Flordaprince, Flordagold or Tropic Snow. The coast does not get the winter chill that traditional peaches need, so high-chill types flower poorly and crop badly there.
Coastal low-chill peaches are among Australia's earliest, with the main crop from mid-October to late November. Granite Belt peaches ripen later, over summer from December to February.

NSW.intro: Growing peaches in New South Wales. NSW spans warm, low-chill coast to cold, high-chill tablelands, so the right peach depends on your district.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: NSW grows both temperate (high-chill) and low-chill stone fruit, and growers pick the variety to match the chill, soil and water of their area. The warm Sydney basin and coast suit low-chill types, while the cool Central Tablelands supply plenty of chill for traditional varieties. Match the variety to your district and peaches are reliable across most of the state.

sections.heading: Where peaches grow in NSW
sections.body: Bilpin in the Hawkesbury is a famous fruit-growing district close to Sydney, and the cool Central Tablelands around Orange, Bathurst and Young grow high-chill stone fruit. For commercial orchards, the irrigated Riverina (the Murrumbidgee Irrigation Area around Griffith and Leeton) is a major producer of peaches and nectarines.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: Warm coastal and low-chill orchards start picking in late spring, from about November, while the cooler tablelands such as Orange ripen later, running into February and March. The state's spread of climates gives NSW one of the longest peach seasons in the country.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is native to the NSW coast and has spread through much of the inland, so it is the main pest of peaches; the irrigated Riverina runs area-wide fruit-fly management to protect its orchards. Leaf curl and brown rot turn up in wet springs, so keep to the late-winter leaf-curl spray and prune for good airflow.

faqs.q: Where are peaches grown in NSW?
faqs.a: From Bilpin and the Sydney basin (low-chill) to the cool Central Tablelands around Orange and Bathurst (high-chill), plus the big commercial orchards of the irrigated Riverina around Griffith and Leeton.

faqs.q: Do I need to manage fruit fly on peaches in NSW?
faqs.a: Yes. Queensland fruit fly is established across most of NSW and readily attacks peaches. Net the tree or use baiting, and pick up fallen fruit promptly to break the breeding cycle.

VIC.intro: Growing peaches in Victoria. Victoria's cold winters give peaches all the chill they need, and the state grows the great bulk of Australia's peaches and nectarines.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Victoria's cool temperate winters easily supply the chill that traditional high-chill peaches want, and its warm, dry summers, especially inland, ripen excellent fruit. The main watch-out is frost, which can damage early blossom and young fruit, so early-flowering varieties need a sheltered, frost-aware spot.

sections.heading: Where peaches grow in VIC
sections.body: The Goulburn Valley around Shepparton, Cobram, Kyabram and Tatura is Australia's stone-fruit heartland and the home of its canning industry, while the hot, irrigated Sunraysia around Mildura and Swan Hill grows early-season fruit. The clingstone canning peach Tatura 204 was bred at Tatura here, a reminder that this is the centre of Australian stone-fruit research.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Early districts like Sunraysia start in late spring, and the Goulburn Valley main crop runs through summer. Mid and late varieties, such as the canning peach Tatura 204, ripen from January into March, carrying Victoria's season well into autumn.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is managed area-wide across Victoria's fruit districts, including the cross-border Greater Sunraysia Pest Free Area, so growers there follow strict baiting and produce-movement rules. Brown rot causes serious losses in wet weather near harvest, and frost protection for early blossom is part of the yearly routine.

faqs.q: Why is Victoria so good for peaches?
faqs.a: Its cold winters easily supply the chill that traditional peaches need, and its warm, dry summers ripen high-quality fruit. Victoria grows the majority of Australia's peaches and nectarines, centred on the Goulburn Valley.
A yellow, clingstone canning peach bred at Tatura in Victoria's Goulburn Valley. It is self-fertile and ripens late, around late February into March, and holds its shape well when bottled.
